text:
The Paris Olympics may have got off to a rocky opening ceremony start but Team GB has had a great Games in 2024 so far, with a haul of 38 medals including 10 golds so far, putting us sixth in the overall table well ahead of the likes of Germany, Japan and Ireland.

But one thing manyOlympicsviewers have noticed on theBBCis that the competitors - whether it’s Max Whitlock, Alex Yee or Beth Potter, have all been competing for ‘Team Great Britain and Northern Ireland’, according to commentators on the live BBC coverage.

But how can it be Team GB if it includes Northern Ireland - and why isn’t it Team UK, then?

READ MORE:Clarkson's Farm fans work out Kaleb Cooper's 'new TV show' cryptic tease

Unlike in the football, in which there’s an historic agreement established between FIFA/UEFA and England, Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland, mostly because the home nations were playing international fixtures with their own Football Associations which predate FIFA and UEFA, the Olympics does not recognise the individual nation-states within the UK.

Instead, the UK puts forward one combined team for the country - and then called it Team GB, which viewers have noticed.

Geographically, Great Britain only refers to the mainland landmass of England, Scotland and Wales and does not include Northern Ireland. But Northern Irish athletes are part of Team GB unless they choose to compete for the Republic of Ireland.

u/Brainfreeze asked onReddit: “Team GB. Why not Team UK? It seems as though NI gets left out. In terms of geography Great Britain is the name of an island.”

One poster replied: “Officially it’s the Great Britain and Northern Ireland Olympic Team. Team GB was picked in 1999 as a snappy marketing tool.”

@saltire_blue said: “Similar to why the England and Wales cricket team is called simply “England.” They claimed it was marketing reasons to whitewash Wales out the team name and the governing body.”

@ducra replied: “But it is no more snappy than Team UK, is it?”

Then, @orgpenguin explained: “It goes back to when the British Olympic Committee was set up yonks ago to cover all British Isles and a number of other territories, including what is now the Irish Republic. The Olympic identity since then has been “GBR”, which evolved into Team GB. I understand the BOC argue that because the team still covers the likes of Jersey, Channel Islands and such that Team UK would be even more inaccurate.”



The decision was taken in 1999, after the British Olympic Association director of marketing decided that the official Great Britain and Northern Ireland Olympic team name was too much of a mouthful, and she masterminded the Team GB concept.

The name has since attracted criticism, with one Northern Irish minister claiming the name “excludes and alienates” people from Northern Ireland.

The BOA has rejected the name Team UK, because Team UK does not cover all members either (indeed, Jersey and the Channel Islands compete in Team GB but are not in the UK).

As the BBC reported: “The BOA has argued that neither the term Team GB nor Team UK are strictly accurate since some members are geographically part of neither Great Britain nor the United Kingdom - for example the Isle of Man, Jersey and some UK overseas territories.”

title: Olympics viewers only just realising why it’s called Team GB not UK | UK | News | Express.co.uk
author: Alex Evans
description: Olympics viewers have been scratching their heads while watching the BBC coverage announcing 'Team GB and Northern Ireland.'
